## Limitations and Future Research
<!-- [PLACEHOLDER] -->

### Order-Book Execution Realism Constraint
<!-- [PLACEHOLDER] -->

A central limitation of the current empirical setup is the use of a
mid-price-like execution proxy for portfolio valuation and reward computation.
This assumption is convenient for controlled RL training, but it is not fully
realistic for live order-book trading.

In production settings, a strategy that demands immediate execution typically
crosses the spread, which introduces a mechanical cost on each trade. Realized
results are also affected by maker/taker fees, queue priority, partial fills,
and latency-dependent adverse selection. These frictions are only partially
represented in the present setup.

Relatedly, the current environment assumes zero market impact: agent trades do
not shift prices or deplete liquidity in a way that changes subsequent
execution conditions. This is reasonable only for small orders relative to
available depth. Relaxing this assumption would likely lower net returns,
increase drawdowns, and penalize high-turnover behavior through impact-driven
slippage and adverse selection.

The setup also assumes zero latency between observation and execution. This is
acceptable for the present research objective because the thesis does not target
latency-arbitrage deployment; however, real HFT environments are highly
latency-sensitive. Some signals that appear profitable in simulation may decay
before execution in live markets, making them effectively non-tradeable.
Additionally, real venues can reject or partially reject orders due to
insufficient margin, stale quotes, risk limits, or rapid order-book changes,
which can further degrade realized performance relative to backtest results.

The practical implication is that reported backtest performance is likely
optimistic relative to deployable net performance. Strategies with high turnover
are especially sensitive.

At tick frequency, spread-crossing costs can be of
similar magnitude to the short-horizon price moves the agent attempts to
capture. This means that a strategy appearing profitable at mid-price may generate
neutral or negative returns under realistic bid/ask execution. Future research
should therefore evaluate the same agents under explicit bid/ask execution
rules, calibrated fee schedules, and slippage/impact models to quantify the
robustness of the observed edge under realistic microstructure constraints.

### Agent-State Feature Extensions
<!-- [PLACEHOLDER] -->

The current observation vector includes the agent's own portfolio exposure as
the sole agent-specific feature. A natural extension is to enrich this with
additional inventory-aware signals that describe the agent's situation within
the episode rather than the external market state. Candidate features include
unrealized P&L on the current position. This would allow the agent to
distinguish a winning long from a losing long. It could also potentially implement
implicit stop-loss or profit-taking behavior.

Other candidates include the number of steps the current
position has been held, which enables time-based exit logic. A rolling
turnover count would make the agent aware of its own recent transaction cost
footprint. At a higher level, the agent's drawdown from its episode peak could
support risk-sensitive behavior during losing runs without requiring explicit
rule-based overlays. These extensions leave the market observation unchanged
and add only internal state, making them a low-disruption way to test whether
richer self-awareness improves policy quality.

### Temporal Context and State Aliasing
<!-- [PLACEHOLDER] -->

The current observation vector is a snapshot: it represents the state of the
order book and the agent's position at a single point in time, with no
explicit encoding of the path that led there. This creates a state aliasing
problem. Two market configurations that are identical in their current
snapshot — same mid-price, same spread, same order flow imbalance, same
portfolio exposure — may have arrived from very different trajectories: one
following a sustained directional move, the other following a reversal. The
economically appropriate response to these two situations is different, yet the
agent receives the same observation and is expected to produce the same action.

Adding lagged values of the key state variables — for instance, a fixed-length
window of past order flow imbalance, mid-price returns, and spread readings —
would partially resolve this. Even a shallow lag stack of five to ten
time steps would allow the agent to distinguish an accelerating trend from
a decelerating one, or a spread that is widening from one that is compressing,
using only the features already present in the current design. The cost is a
proportional increase in state dimensionality, which adds sample complexity
to training but does not require architectural changes. This is a practical
first step that could be evaluated independently of the more substantial
redesign implied by sequence encoders.

The architectural answer to state aliasing is to replace the flat observation
vector with a learned temporal representation — an RNN hidden state or a
Transformer attention window — as discussed in the alternative architectures
section below. But the simpler diagnostic is to introduce raw lags first: if
adding a modest lag window produces a measurable improvement in policy
stability and out-of-sample performance, that result would directly motivate
the more complex sequence architecture. If it does not, the aliasing problem
may be less binding than the missing information sources discussed in the
following section.

### Partial Observability and the Limits of the Spot Order Book
<!-- [PLACEHOLDER] -->

A fundamental constraint of the current setup — and of most academic RL
trading research — is that the agent operates under severe partial
observability. The state representation is constructed entirely from
the spot limit order book of a single instrument at a single venue.
This is a narrow window onto a market that is simultaneously shaped by
activity across multiple execution venues, cross-asset dynamics, and
information sources that have no representation in the LOB at all.

Partial observability matters because agents trained on incomplete state
representations are forced to treat phenomena that have external causes as
unexplained noise in the environment. A price move driven by a large options
hedge, a macro news release, or an institutional block trade executed in a
dark pool appears to the agent as an unpredictable shock to the order book
rather than as the consequence of an observable event. This limits the
precision of any value estimate the agent can form and raises the variance
of the reward signal, both of which degrade policy quality.

Two categories of missing information are particularly relevant at the
short horizons studied here. The first is the derivatives complex.
Linear derivatives such as index futures and ETFs carry anticipatory price
information because they trade with lower friction and are often the
preferred instrument for expressing directional views. Non-linear derivatives
— most importantly the single-stock and index options markets — embed
forward-looking information about the distribution of future returns rather
than just direction. Movements in implied volatility, put/call skew, and the
term structure of options prices frequently precede movements in the
underlying spot market, and they capture the aggregate assessment of
informed participants about tail risk and regime transitions. An observation
vector that incorporates even a compact representation of the near-term
implied volatility surface — at-the-money vol, skew, and term structure slope
— would give the agent access to information that is currently invisible to
it, at the cost of substantially increasing state dimensionality and
data infrastructure requirements.

The second category is unstructured data and its downstream signals.
Earnings announcements, central bank communications, analyst revisions,
regulatory filings, and macroeconomic releases all move prices at horizons
that are relevant to intraday positioning. Processing this information — which
arrives as text, audio, or structured releases rather than as time series
— requires natural language processing pipelines that sit entirely outside
the scope of the current framework. A richer agent design would integrate
processed signals from these sources: event indicators, sentiment scores, or
embedding-based representations of document content. The practical challenge
is that the quality and latency of such signals varies enormously across
providers, and constructing a clean, backtest-safe version of an NLP-derived
feature set introduces its own lookahead and selection biases that must be
controlled carefully.

Together, these limitations suggest that the performance ceiling of any
agent that sees only the spot LOB is bounded not by the choice of RL
algorithm or reward function, but by the information content of its
observation. Closing this gap incrementally — first by adding futures and
ETF quote data, then compact options market features, then processed
text signals — would allow future work to decompose how much of the
remaining unexplained return variance is attributable to each missing
information source.

### Cross-Asset and Multi-Asset Extensions
<!-- [PLACEHOLDER] -->

The empirical design uses a pooled model trained across six Nasdaq large-cap
equities (AAPL, MSFT, TSLA, META, AMZN, and AVGO), but the agent still manages a
single-asset position at a time. A direct extension is to introduce synchronized cross-asset
observations: intraday VIX futures as a regime-conditioning signal, sector ETF
order flow as a proxy for institutional activity, or correlated large-cap LOB
states that may lead price discovery. A more substantial extension is to
move from single-asset exposure control to genuine multi-asset portfolio optimization,
where the agent simultaneously manages positions across multiple instruments and
must account for correlation, capital constraints, and cross-asset execution
costs.

A more tractable intermediate step — and a stronger test of whether the agent
can learn to exploit a structured relationship — would be to design the
simulation around instrument pairs with an economically well-defined price
anchor rather than opportunistic correlation. The dual-class share structure of
Alphabet Inc. offers a natural candidate: GOOGL (Class A shares, carrying one
vote per share) and GOOG (Class C shares, carrying no voting rights) represent
identical economic exposure to the same underlying business and trade on the
same exchange. In the absence of corporate events affecting voting rights, both
classes should track the same fundamental value, and historical deviations
between their prices are generally small, bounded, and mean-reverting.

This setting is structurally different from predicting the direction of a single
asset's price. An agent managing a relative position — long one class and short
the other — has access to a signal with a known theoretical anchor: any
persistent spread between GOOG and GOOGL is either an arbitrage or a
compensation for the difference in voting power. The target is the spread, not
the level. This makes the learning problem more tractable, the evaluation
criterion cleaner, and the behavioral interpretation of the learned policy
more transparent. Whether a RL agent trained on LOB data can learn to
identify and close such deviations — and whether that edge survives execution
costs in a market where this spread is actively monitored — would be a more
decisive test of the approach than the current single-asset directional
setup.

### Narrow Evaluation Window and Scope of the Empirical Claim
<!-- [PLACEHOLDER] -->

The most important constraint on interpreting the empirical results is the
three-day evaluation window. The training, validation, and test splits all
derive from the same three consecutive trading sessions (February 25–27,
2026). What the thesis describes as "out-of-sample" performance is therefore
within-window out-of-sample: the test split was not used during training, but
it belongs to the same short market episode, characterized by the same
volatility regime, the same spread environment, and the same intraday
seasonality as the training data. The agent has never encountered a different
trading day, let alone a different market condition or instrument.

This distinction matters for what the results actually support. The empirical
findings are best read as a viability test. The pipeline can extract
useful signal from order-book features and translate this into a policy that
outperforms benchmarks within a controlled, narrow window. This is a necessary
condition for practical utility, but not a sufficient one. These conclusions would be undermined if the agent had failed to
beat benchmarks even in this favorable setting. What it does not establish is
whether the learned policy would remain profitable across a broader range of
market conditions, or even across a separate three-day window of data
with different volatility or flow characteristics.

Extending the evaluation window to cover multiple weeks of data, including
periods of elevated volatility, low liquidity, and structural breaks, would
be the most direct way to strengthen the generalization claim. Walk-forward
validation — re-training on an expanding window and evaluating on successive
held-out periods — would further separate temporal generalization from
in-window interpolation. Cross-asset testing would include foreign exchange, futures,
and cryptocurrency. This would establish whether the feature design and training
protocol are robust to structural differences in order-book dynamics. It would also determine
whether asset-class-specific tuning is necessary. These extensions are
identified as the primary experimental directions for future work.

### Algorithm Comparison and Reward Ablation
<!-- [PLACEHOLDER] -->

The empirical pipeline is structured around TD3 and the Differential Sharpe
Ratio reward. Two natural ablation axes remain unexplored. First, replacing
TD3 with SAC — which maximizes a maximum-entropy objective and tends to
maintain broader exploration throughout training — or with PPO would test
whether the deterministic actor-critic family is genuinely best suited to this
problem. Second, replacing the DSR reward with alternatives such as a
Sortino-based signal, a direct log-return objective, or a reward that
explicitly penalizes drawdown would test the sensitivity of policy behavior to
reward specification — a dimension the thesis identifies as important but does
not empirically vary.

### Alternative Model Architectures
<!-- [PLACEHOLDER] -->

Two architectural directions appear promising. First, replacing
the MLP actor and critic with a Transformer-based sequence encoder would allow
the agent to attend selectively over a window of past order-book states rather
than acting on a single flattened snapshot. Transformer architectures have shown
strong performance on sequential financial data [@Vaswani2017], and their
attention mechanism aligns well with LOB inputs where the relative
importance of recent events varies with market conditions.

Second, a hybrid approach combining gradient-boosted trees with the RL framework
is worth investigating. The recently proposed GBRL framework [@Fuhrer2025]
directly integrates gradient boosting into the actor-critic architecture,
replacing neural network components with gradient-boosted trees. The value
estimation step is effectively a regression on tabular microstructure features.
This is a natural fit for tree-based models known to perform well on structured
financial data. Additionally, the interpretability of individual trees may provide
additional insight into which features drive the learned value function.

### Evaluation Robustness
<!-- [PLACEHOLDER] -->

The current results are based on a fixed train/test split and a single random
seed. Walk-forward validation — expanding the training window and re-evaluating
on successive out-of-sample periods — would provide a more rigorous assessment
of temporal generalization. Multi-seed reporting with confidence intervals
would separate genuine policy quality from favorable initialization, which is a
known confound in RL research.

### Compute Scale as a Prerequisite for Superior Performance
<!-- [PLACEHOLDER] -->

A structural limitation of the present work — and of most academic RL trading
research — is the scale at which models are trained and evaluated. The
experiments in this thesis were conducted on a single machine with a modest GPU
budget, covering six instruments over three trading sessions. This is a
reasonable scope for a proof-of-concept study, but it raises the question of
whether the marginal gains observed here could be converted into robust,
economically meaningful edge at deployment scale.

There is reason to believe that the relationship between compute and
performance is not merely incremental in this domain. Profitably operating
at tick frequency requires forecasting models that generalize across
instruments, regimes, and time horizons simultaneously. Training such models
demands large-scale data ingestion, distributed feature computation, and
the ability to run many parallel rollouts — a workload that is qualitatively
different from fitting a model to a few days of data on one asset.

The capital expenditure decisions of leading quantitative firms suggest this
view is widely held among practitioners. XTX Markets, a firm generating
approximately \$250 billion in daily trading volume across equities, FX, fixed
income, and derivatives, announced in January 2025 a commitment of over
\euro{}1 billion to build a dedicated machine learning data centre campus in
Kajaani, Finland, with the stated purpose of housing the infrastructure for its
price-forecasting models across more than 50,000 instruments.^[See
@XTXKajaani2025. The first phase covers 22.5 MW of IT capacity, with four
additional data centres planned on the same 478-acre site.] Similarly, Qube
Research \& Technologies (QRT), one of Europe's largest quantitative hedge
funds, announced in February 2025 the construction of a dedicated
high-performance computing facility in Akureyri, Iceland — powered by
renewable geothermal and hydroelectric energy — specifically for accelerated
compute supporting large-scale model calibration and data analysis.^[See
@QRTIceland2025. QRT managed approximately \$23 billion in AUM at the time of
the announcement.]

These investments are not incidental infrastructure decisions. They reflect an
industry consensus that superior ML-driven performance at the frontier of
quantitative trading is contingent on compute scale that is far beyond what
any single academic research project can access. Future academic work in this
area should therefore be interpreted with this constraint in mind: the ceiling
of what is achievable under typical academic compute budgets is likely well
below the performance that would be observed under industry-scale training
regimes. Collaborative research arrangements, cloud-based large-scale
experimentation, or partnerships with data providers may be necessary to
close this gap.
